CalifOrnia
Fair POlitical
Practices Commission
October 
■8′  ■99■
David H. Kirkpatrick, Attorney at Law
National Economic Developnent & Law Center
1950 Addison Street
Berkeley, cA 94704
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I -91-4 41
Dear l.lr. Kirkpatrick:
we have received your letter of Septenrber 11, 1991, in which
you seek genera]. advice on behalf of the south Berkeley Cornmunity
Housing Developnent corporation regarding its duties and
respons ibi I it ies under the conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the
PoliticaL Reform Act (the rrActrr),1 and specifically whether it is
a 'rLocal government agencyrr pursuant to section 82041. As the
authorized representative of the south Berkeley Cornmunity Housing
Developnent Corporation, we provide you l./ith advice pursuant to
Regulation L8329, but as your request for advice does not refer to
a specific pending governmental decision, we are treating your
request as one for informal assistance only. 
(Regulation
1si2e(b).)2
The following advice is based upon the facts provided in your
Ietter, your fo11ow-up letter of october 1, 1991 in response to
our request for additional information, and in a telephone
conversation with Margorie GeIb of the Berkeley city Attorney's
Office on September 23, 1991.
・  Government Code sections 8■000-910■5.  All statutory references
are to the Covernment Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 Californ■a Code of Regulations Section
■8000′  et seo.  All references to regulations are to Title 2′
Div■s■on 6 of the Californ■a Code of Regulations.
2  1nformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(SeCtiOn 83■■4′  Regulation 18329(c)(3)。
)
428 J Street,Suite 800 0 P.0.Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804‐ 0807 ● (916)322・5660


FiIe No. I -91-4 41
Page 2
OUESTIONS
(1) Does the South Berkeley Community Housing Development
corporation continue to remain a 'rlocaI governnent agencyrr for
purposes of the Act?
(2) what circumstances might change the status of the south
Berkeley conmunity Housing Developrnent corporation?
CONCLUSION
(1) On the basis of the facts you have presented, the South
Berkeley conmunity Housing Develop:nent corporation continues to
rernain a rrlocal. government agency'r for purposes of the Act.
(2) while an abstract discussion of circumstances that night
change the local government agency status of an entity is
hypothetical and therefore not one about which advice can be
provided, an evaluation under the standards set forth in the
Connission's opinion in In re Sieqel (L977) 3 FPPC ops. 62 will
determine the local- governnent agency status.
FACTS
In 1988, the Fair Political. Pract j.ces Commission (r'the
connission") advised the city Attorney of the city of Berkeley
that the South Berkeley comlnunity Housing Development Corporatlon
("the corporationrr) was a rrlocal governnent agencyrr for purposes
of the Act, and that the activities of ttpublic of f icialsrl
aff j.Iiated with the corporation were subject to the financial
disclosure and disqua I i fication provisions of the Act.
(Albuouerque Advice Letter, No. A-88-422) (riAlbuquerque letter. r')
This idvice was prernised on the apptication of the criteria
delineated in the Com:nission's opinion in In re SieqeL (L977) 3
FPPC ops. 62 ('rsieqel'r) to deterrnine whether an entity was public
or private for purposes of the Act.
To conclude that an entity is a locaL government entity under
the Act, SieqeI applied four criteria:
(1) r.rhether the inpetus for the formation of the entity
originated wj.th a government agency;
(2) whether the entity was substantially funded by, or its
prinary source of funds r./as, a government agency.
(3) vrhether a principal purpose for formation of the entity
r^/as to provide servi.ces or undertake obli.gations which public
agencies are legally and traditionally authorized to perform; and
(4) whether the entity r./as treated as a public entity by
other statutory provisions.


FiIe No. I -91-4 41
Page 3
In the Albuquerque letter, the Comnission found that the
fulfillnent 
of the first three criteria was sufficient to conclude
that the corporation was a public entity. 
First, the city,s
attorneys drafted the documents by which the corporation was
forned, after accepting the reconmendations of a citizen committee
that the city forned to make such recommendat ions ; therefore, the
first criteria of sieqel vras met. Second, the city l-oaned
interest-free funds to the corporation, agreed to purchase the
real property on which the corporation would develop housing and
then lease back to the corporation for one dollar for 55 yearsr
after which the ownership of the developnent $rould vest in the
city; therefore, the second criteria of Sieqel $ras met. Third,
the corporation was formed for the purpose of providing adequate
1ow and moderate lncome housing, a legaI and traditional
obligation of public agencies,' therefore, the third criteria of
Sieqel was net.
In your Ietter you indicate that "circumstances have changedrr
from when the advice in the Albuoueroue letter was provided. You
have identified two such changes:
(1) while the corporation's original board of directors was
forrned fron the city-appointed conmittee, only five of the
originaJ. board rnembers renain and the city does not participate in
the selection of new board nembers
(2, while the corporation's original and sole source of
financial support was a loan fron the city, fund j.ng from the city
now cornprises approximately 38t of the total develoPment costs,
exclusive of the city's providing the land to the corporation
under the one do1lar, fifty-five 
year 1ease.
You have suggested that not aII, but only a portion, of the
housing units to be facilitated by the corporation was designed to
be ttaffordable,rt and that the project was always intended to be a
nixed income one, with more than half of the units set at market
rate. This infornation item is not different from what was
provided to us prevj.ously, as part of the facts on $/hj.ch the
Albuqueroue letter was prenised. You have updated the
distribution of the rnix of very 1o!, (Iess than 5oZ of county
median incone) and low (Iess than 8Ot of county median income)
income units, noting that 30* - and not 20* - of the units are
targeted for very low, and 14t - not 20t - of the units are
targeted for low, incorne units.
Finalty, you have j.ndicated that the corporation's
"jurisdiction" 
consists of one project property on which aII of
the housinq units are located; the project property at present
includes a total of. 43 units, with an anticipated occupancy of L05
individuals.


FiIe No. I -91-4 41
Page 4
ANALYSIS
A public official 
is prohibited from making, particj-pating
in, or using his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which the official 
knows or has reason to
kno!, he or she has a financial interest. 
(Section 87100.) This
prohibition acconpl.ishes one the Act's express purposes: the
disclosure by public officials of assets and incorne that may be
affected by their official 
actions, and disqua I if ication of the
officials 
fron participation in certain decisions so as to avoid
conflicts of interest. 
(See Section 81002(c).) The Act also
seeks to prevent the appearance of a possible conflict of interest
as we1I. (Witt v. Morro!, (L977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 823,')
A public official 
is defined as a member, officer, 
employee
or consultant of a state or local government agency. (Section
82048.) A rloca1 governrnent agencyrr is defined as rta county, city
or district 
of any kind including school district, 
or any other
IocaI or regiona). political subdivision, or any department,
division, bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the
foregoing." (section 82o4l-, emphasis added. )
In the Albuquerque Ietter we advised that application of the
Sieqet criteria 
was necessary to deternine whether the corporation
qualified as an itother agency" of the city of Berkeley, as the
definition of locaI government agency in Section 82041 did not
adequately resolve the question raised by the corporation.
Prior to the Albuouerque letter the criteria set forth in
Sieqel had been applied by the Cornmission to determine whether an
entity was public or private under the Act. (In re Leach (L978) 4
FPPC Ops. 48; Francis Advice Letter, No. A-86-214i Hopkins Advice
Letter, No, A-81-38.) The conmission has also continued to apply
the Sieqe1 criteria 
subsequent to the Albuquerque Ietter to
determine whether an entity was public or private. 
(Lesser Advice
Letter, No. A-91-305,' Snow Advice Letter, No. I-90-728; Lober
Advice Letter, No. A-90-135; Keene Advice Letter, No. I-89-613;
Schofield Advice Letter, No. A-89-540.)
Therefore, the deternination of whether the corporation is or
is not a local governnent agency continues to be subject to
evaluation under the sieqel criteria. 
Eor the reasons discussed
as fo1lows, it is our conclusion that the information you have
provided does not change our prj.or deterrnination that the
corporatj-on remains a rrlocal governnent agencyrr pursuant to
Section a2o47, and that the board nembers are |tpublic officials"
pursuant to Section 82048 for purposes of the Act's disclosure and
disqua 1i f ication provisions. This :neans that as pubJ.ic officials
the board members must disqualify thenselves from any corporation
decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on them that is distinguishable from the effect
on the public Aenerally. (Section 87L03.)


File No. I -91-4 41
Page 5
The Sieqel Criteria
(1) whether the inpetus for fornation of the corporation
oridinated rrith a oovernment aoency
fn the Albuoueroue letter rde concluded that [it 
was
undoubtedly the caserr that the impetus for fornation of the
corporation originated with the City of Berkeley.
With respect to this criterion, you have acknowledged that no
ne!, facts exist, other than the change in board membership and
appointment authority. 
Nej.ther the board membership nor the
appointnent authority are factors which irnpact the impetus for
forrnation. Therefore, we see no reason to change the previous
conclusion that this component of the SieqeI requirements
continues to be net.
You suggest that the rri.mpetus requirement" of Sieqel would
tthistorically indicate some form of control over the
organization,rr and that in view of the current board membership
and appointment authority, the city no longer rrcontrolsrr the
corporation.
We rejected the suggestion that the focus be on whether a
government agency exerts control over the organization in question
lrhen you previously proposed it. 
(Kirkpatrick Advice Letter, No.
I-89-150.) The rejection was premised, inter alia, on the
understanding that such a focus would be di.fficult to implement
and $rould serve to encourage governnent agencies to forn
ttindependent" organizations providing traditional governnental
services so as to avoid the requirenents of the Act. You have not
offered any argunent to suggest that the conclusion should be
reeva luated .
(2) whether the corporation is substantiallv funded by. or
its prinarv source of funds is, a qovernment aoencv
Initially, 
the sole source of financial support for the
corporation lras a loan fron the city of Berkeley. In the
Albuquerque letter we concluded that the city was the
corporation's prinary source of funding because, in addj,tion to
the city's S225,000 loan to the corporation, the city leased to
the corporation the real property upon which the development was
to take place for one dollar for fifty-five 
years, wi,th ownership
of the development to vest in the city when the lease expired,
This permitted the conclusj.on that the city was the prj,mary source
of funding for the corporatj.on.
You have inforned us that funding from the city now comprises
approxirnately 338 of the total developnent costs ($1,729,154 o]ul'
of a total development cost of $5,259.154). We note, however,
that the city continues to be the single largest fundj.ng source
for the corporation. Uoreover, your computation of the total


Fil.e No. r-91-4 41
Page 6
development costs fails to account for the dollar value of the
other factors that were determinative in, and appear to remain
unchanged since, the Albuquercrue letter: the interest-free, 
one
dollar, fifty-five 
year loan (a leasehoLd interest whi.ch you have
valued at approximately $200,000), and the furnishing of aIl- real
property upon which the corporation wilL build housing (property
whose acquisition costs were estinated at approxinately
$1,700,000).
The value of all benefits provided by the city to the
corporation, including the actual funding and the "in-kind"
advintages, clearly demonstrate that, at ninirnurn, the city
continues to substintially fund the corporation- The addition of
other funding sources is obviously a new factor requiring
evaluation. Having done so, we see no reason to change the
previous conclusion that this component of the Sieqel requj-rements
lontinues to be met, because the qorporation continues to be
substantially funded by the city.3 
we decline to accept your
suggestion that the teims "substantially funded by" and "primary
f under'r are synonynous .
(3) whether one of the DrinciDal purposes for the fgrmation
oi the corporation is to provide services or undertake
3I., th" current funding sources breakdown you have indicated that
the corporation has received a $1,280,000 loan fron the State of
california, or approximately 24* of existi.ng funding sources.
Therefore, alnost 6ot of the corporation's funding presently comes
from governnental entities.
The Albuquerque letter concluded that because the provision
of adequaGlous ing f or low and moderate j'ncome f ami I ies was an
obliga€ion that public agencies have been Ieqally and
traditionally 
authorized to perforro, this criterion of SieoeI had
been satisfild. 
This conclusion was supported by the fact that
Governtnent Code Section 37364, under v/hich the corporati-on was
f orrned, permits cities to utilize real property to provide
affordabie housing to low and moderate incone families'
rn your initial 
letter you have acknowledged that no new
facts exist with respect to evaluation of this criterion'
However, you have suggested that ttonly a portj'on 9f the units from
the begi,n;ing were designed to be affordable,r' and that the
projec€ has ilways been intended to be a rnixed income one, with
more than hatf of the units set at narket rate.
we are not unfaniliar with this infornation; it was provided
previously by the Berkeley city Attorney in 1988, who informed us
tf,at tne bisposition and Development Agreenent concluded between
the city and the corporation spLcified that the rnix of housing
units to be rented wls distributed 2ot - 2oZ - 50% to famil'ies


File No. I-91-4 41
Page 7
with incornes of no more than 5Ot, 8O*, and 12Ot of the county
rnedian, respectively.
Government code Section 373G4 does not mandate any percentage
or proportional reguirenents with respect to the city,! ieal
property vis-a-vis the number of affordable housing units for 1ow
and moderate income faroilies. 
For that reason, in the Albuouerque
Ietter, the fact that the housing nix to be achieved inctuaea a
low and noderate incone component tas sufficient for purposes of
satisfying the third SieqeI criterion. 
We see no reason to change
the conclusion with respect to this Sieqel criterion, that the
corporation was formed for the principal purpose of providing a
servj.ce or otherwise undertaking an obLigation which the city is
Iegally and traditionally 
authorized to perforrn: providing
affordable housing for low and noderate income families. 
!,JhiIe
you have acknowledged in your subsequent letter that the actual-
percentage of the combined very lor.r and 1ow incorne units is now
43t, rather than 40t, of the total units, (that 30? rather than
20t of the units are set aside for very 1o!, income units, and l4Z
rather than 20t are designated for 1ow incorne units), these
changes do not appear significant given the lack of any mandated
percentage or proportionality requirements in Government Code
Section 37364. Therefore, we reaffirm our conclusion on this
point.
(4) Whether the COrporation is treated as a public entity bv
other_statutory provisions
The Albuquerque letter noted that application of this
criterion to the corporation was I'problenat ic . rr The corporation
appeared to be a public benefit corporation formulated under the
Nonprofit Public Benefit Corporation Lar, (Corporations Code
Section 511.0 et seo. ) and thus treated as a tax exempt entity.
Holrever, nothing else about the corporation indicated that it was
treated the sarne as public entities by other statutory provisions.
The letter concluded that satisfaction of this criterion was not
necessary for the conclusion that the corporation was a 1ocal
government agency under the Act, in view of the satisfaction of
the other three criteria.
You have not offered any ne!, circumstances which would bring
the corporation within this criterion, and r./e have no basis for
concluding differently from that which was concluded in the
Albuquerque letter on this point. Similarly, lre do not bel-ieve
that the present failure to satisfy the fourth SieqeI cri-terion
disturbs the determination that the corporation is a local
government aqency, as the other three criteria are satisfied.
Because we beU.eve the corporation continues to satisfy the
first three criteria of Sieqel, we conclude that the corporation
continues to be a loca] government agency. The changed
circumstances that you,ve offered are insufficient to warrant
modification of this conclusion.


File No. I -91-4 41
Page I
confLict of Interest and the t'PubIic Generallv" Exception
In your letter you express concern that should the
corporation be regarded as a local government agency, it faces the
prospect of being unable to function because more board menbers,
who are residents of the corporation,s property, will have
disqualifying conflicts of interest under the Act even though no
conflict would exist under the Corporations Code.
The conmission does not advise vrith respect to provisions of
the ]ar., other than the Act; therefore, we cannot conment on your
interpretation of the Corporations code. with respect to your
concern, however, we direct your attention to two applications of
the t'public AeneraIIy't exception as described in Commission
Regulations 18702 (c) , 18703, and 18703. 1.
Under the exception as provided by Regulations 18702(c) and
18703, public official.s with financial interests that wiIl be
rnaterially affect by a decision nay nevertheless participate in
the decision if the decision wiII affect the officials' 
interests
in substantially the same manner as it will affect all members of
the public or a significant segrment of the publj,c.
The "pubIic" consists of aIl persons residing, ownj,ng
property, or doing business, in the jurisdiction of the agency in
question. (In re Leqan, (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1,'In re Ohlen (1976) 2
FPPC ops. 77; churton Advice Letter, No. I-91-321.) In the case
of the corporation, this would be the entire property area(s)
included within the corporation's jurisdiction. 
(Moe Advice
Letter, No. A-89-454; chin Advice Letter, No. A-88-091.)
You have indicated that a single property on sacranento
Street in Berkeley presently cornprises the corporation's
jurisdiction. 
The project consists of 43 units trith an expected
total occupancy of 105 individuals. 
It is also understood that
the mernbers of the corporation's board nust be residents of the
project, and that residents are tenants, not owners, of the
property in vhich they reside.
Given the srnall number of individuals who reside in the
jurisdiction 
and the unique sirnilarities of the residency
requirernents, it is clear that the decisions nade by the members
of the corporation's board will in sone instances affect their
interests in substantially the sane manner as it wilI affect a1I,
or a significant segment, of the residents wj.thin the jurisdiction
of the corporation.
when such j.s the case, the exception provided by Regulation
18703 would be appl).cable and the board nernbers would not be
precluded frorn participating in the decision. we therefore do not
concur erith your suggestion that the board will be unable to


File No. I -91-4 41
Page 9
function by virtue of being rrtarred $rith the status of a Local
government agency,rr and instead suqgest that tinely and
appropriate application of Regulation 18703 rnay obviate your
concerns in this regard. r
Under the exception as provided by Regulation 18703.1,
decisions that \.riI1 effect the public official's 
principal
residence do not require the Qisqua I i f ication of the official 
if
certain requirements are met.5 Among other things, this exception
requires that an official's 
principal residence be more than 300
feet frorn the boundaries of a property which is the subject of the
decision. Under the particular facts applicable to the
corporation's property, this reguirement would result in
Regulation 18?03.1 not being applicable. However, it could be
applicable to corporation decisions concerning adjacent or other
pioperties other than those currently under the corporation's
j ur isd j.ction .
The Conmission has never adopted a strict mathematical
formula for deter:nining what constitutes a significant segment of
the public. 
(churton Advj.ce Letter, suDra. ) whether an exception
as piovided by Regulations 18702(c) and 18703, or Regulation
18703.1, is applicabLe dePends on the particular facts of the
decision being made by the official(s) 
.
LeqaLIy Elequired Participat
If the exceptions discussed above are not applicable, board
nenbers otherwisL disqual.ified frora a decision rnay sti1l
participate in a decision if his or her participation is IegaIIy
required. (Section 87101.. )
The Cornmission has interpreted this exception narrowly so as
to permit participation of the fewest financially interested
perlons possible in any decision. (In re Hudson, (L978') 4 FPPC
4 As tenants, board members - by virtue of their leasehold
interest - may encounter potentially disqualifying conflicts in
decisions. 
(See Regulation 18702.4.) To the extent thej'r
Ieasehold interests would be affected in a manner sirnilar to the
other tenants in the property, the exception provided by
Regulation 18703 nay, depending on the facts, still 
facilitate
their participation in the decision.
' A*ong other things, the regulation requires that the popuJ.ation
of the jurisdiction be 25,ooo or less and cover a geographic area
of ten -quare niLes. or less; that the public official 
reside in
ttre jurilaiction; 
if elected, the public official 
be el.ected in an
at-large election; that the decision not have a direct effect
(Regulation L8702. r,(a) (3)) on the public officiat's 
principal
residencel that the principal residence be nore than 300 feet from
the boundaries of the property whlch is the subject of the
decision. 
(Regulation 18073.1(a) (1)-(5).)


FiIe No. I-91-441
Page 10
ops. 13; churton Advice Letter, supra.) Regulation 18701 does not
consider an official's 
participation in a decis j.on to be 't1egally
requiredtr unless there is no alternative source of decision
consistent uith the statute authorizing the decision. In no
instances does the Act perDit an otherwise disqualified official
to participate in a decision to break a tie vote, or if a quorum
can be convened of other menbers of the agency who are not
disqualified, whether or not such other members are actually
present at the tirne of the disgual i fication. 
(Regul-ation
18701(a),(c).) Again, whether an official's 
participation is
trlegatly required'r will depend on the particular facts of the
decision being made by the official(s).
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
rnatter, or if you wish to supplement your advice request with
additional specific facts, please contact ne at (916) 322'59oL.

Scott Hallabrin
counsel, LegaI Divis ion

Eor the reasons discussed
as fo1lows, it is our conclusion that the information you have

confLict of Interest and the t'PubIic Generallv" Exception
In your letter you express concern that should the
corporation be regarded as a local government agency, it faces the
prospect of being unable to function because more board menbers,
who are residents of the corporation,s property, will have
disqualifying conflicts of interest under the Act even though no

The conmission does not advise vrith respect to provisions of
the ]ar., other than the Act; therefore, we cannot conment on your
interpretation of the Corporations code. with respect to your
concern, however, we direct your attention to two applications of
the t'public AeneraIIy't exception as described in Commission
Regulations 18702 (c) , 18703, and 18703. 1.
Under the exception as provided by Regulations 18702(c) and
18703, public official.s with financial interests that wiIl be
rnaterially affect by a decision nay nevertheless participate in
the decision if the decision wiII affect the officials' 
interests
in substantially the same manner as it will affect all members of
the public or a significant segrment of the publj,c.
The "pubIic" consists of aIl persons residing, ownj,ng
property, or doing business, in the jurisdiction of the agency in
question. (In re Leqan, (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1,'In re Ohlen (1976) 2
FPPC ops. 77; churton Advice Letter, No. I-91-321.) In the case
of the corporation, this would be the entire property area(s)
included within the corporation's jurisdiction. 
(Moe Advice
Letter, No. A-89-454; chin Advice Letter, No. A-88-091.)
You have indicated that a single property on sacranento
Street in Berkeley presently cornprises the corporation's
jurisdiction.

total occupancy of 105 individuals.

the mernbers of the corporation's board nust be residents of the

jurisdiction 
and the unique sirnilarities of the residency
requirernents, it is clear that the decisions nade by the members
of the corporation's board will in sone instances affect their
interests in substantially the sane manner as it wilI affect a1I,
or a significant segment, of the residents wj.thin the jurisdiction
of the corporation.
when such j.s the case, the exception provided by Regulation
18703 would be appl).cable and the board nernbers would not be
precluded frorn participating in the decision. we therefore do not
concur erith your suggestion that the board will be unable to

function by virtue of being rrtarred $rith the status of a Local
government agency,rr and instead suqgest that tinely and
appropriate application of Regulation 18703 rnay obviate your
concerns in this regard. r
Under the exception as provided by Regulation 18703.1,
decisions that \.riI1 effect the public official's 
principal
residence do not require the Qisqua I i f ication of the official 
if
certain requirements are met.5 Among other things, this exception
requires that an official's

feet frorn the boundaries of a property which is the subject of the

QUESTION: (1) Does the South Berkeley Community Housing Development
corporation continue to remain a 'rlocaI governnent agencyrr for
purposes of the Act?
(2) what circumstances might change the status of the south
Berkeley conmunity Housing Developrnent corporation?

CONCLUSION: (1) On the basis of the facts you have presented, the South
Berkeley conmunity Housing Develop:nent corporation continues to
rernain a rrlocal. government agency'r for purposes of the Act.
(2) while an abstract discussion of circumstances that night
change the local government agency status of an entity is
hypothetical and therefore not one about which advice can be
provided, an evaluation under the standards set forth in the
Connission's opinion in In re Sieqel (L977) 3 FPPC ops. 62 will
determine the local- governnent agency status.

Kirkpatrick, Attorney at Law National Economic Developnent & Law Center 1950 Addison Street Berkeley, cA 94704 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I -91-4 41 Dear l.lr. Kirkpatrick: we have received your letter of Septenrber 11, 1991, in which you seek genera]. advice on behalf of the south Berkeley Cornmunity Housing Developnent corporation regarding its duties and respons ibi I it ies under the conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the PoliticaL Reform Act (the rrActrr),1 and specifically whether it is a 'rLocal government agencyrr pursuant to section 82041. As the authorized representative of the south Berkeley Cornmunity Housing Developnent Corporation, we provide you l./ith advice pursuant to Regulation L8329, but as your request for advice does not refer to a specific pending governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance only. (Regulation 1si2e(b).)2 The following advice is based upon the facts provided in your Ietter, your fo11ow-up letter of october 1, 1991 in response to our request for additional information, and in a telephone conversation with Margorie GeIb of the Berkeley city Attorney's Office on September 23, 1991. ・ Government Code sections 8■000-910■5. All statutory references are to the Covernment Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 Californ■a Code of Regulations Section ■8000′ et seo. All references to regulations are to Title 2′ Div■s■on 6 of the Californ■a Code of Regulations. 2 1nformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (SeCtiOn 83■■4′ Regulation 18329(c)(3)。 ) 428 J Street,Suite 800 0 P.0.Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804‐ 0807 ● (916)322・5660 FiIe No. I -91-4 41 Page 2 OUESTIONS (1) Does the South Berkeley Community Housing Development corporation continue to remain a 'rlocaI governnent agencyrr for purposes of the Act? (2) what circumstances might change the status of the south Berkeley conmunity Housing Developrnent corporation? CONCLUSION (1) On the basis of the facts you have presented, the South Berkeley conmunity Housing Develop:nent corporation continues to rernain a rrlocal. government agency'r for purposes of the Act. (2) while an abstract discussion of circumstances that night change the local government agency status of an entity is hypothetical and therefore not one about which advice can be provided, an evaluation under the standards set forth in the Connission's opinion in In re Sieqel (L977) 3 FPPC ops. 62 will determine the local- governnent agency status. FACTS In 1988, the Fair Political. Pract j.ces Commission (r'the connission") advised the city Attorney of the city of Berkeley that the South Berkeley comlnunity Housing Development Corporatlon ("the corporationrr) was a rrlocal governnent agencyrr for purposes of the Act, and that the activities of ttpublic of f icialsrl aff j.Iiated with the corporation were subject to the financial disclosure and disqua I i fication provisions of the Act.
